article.title: Diablo IV's Lord of Hatred Expansion Launches With New Skovos Region, Paladin and Warlock Classes, and a Reworked Endgame
article.summary: Blizzard's second Diablo IV expansion arrived April 28, 2026 across PC and consoles, adding two new classes, the Skovos region, and a 3.0 systems overhaul. Critics gave it generally favorable scores while servers buckled under launch demand.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Blizzard Entertainment released *Lord of Hatred*, the second major expansion for *Diablo IV*, on April 28, 2026, capping the Mephisto storyline that began with 2024's *Vessel of Hatred*. The release brought two new playable classes, a previously lore-only region called Skovos, a redesigned endgame system, and a free 3.0.0 base-game patch that touches every player. Aggregate review scores landed in generally favorable territory, while launch-evening queues returned for one of the busiest weekends in the franchise's recent history.

## What Shipped

According to the [official Blizzard announcement](https://news.blizzard.com/en-us/article/24267729/prepare-for-the-reckoning-lord-of-hatred-draws-near), *Lord of Hatred* unlocked at 4:00 p.m. PDT on April 27, with the parallel Season of Reckoning launching 30 minutes later. The expansion is available on PlayStation 4, PlayStation 5, Xbox One, Xbox Series X|S, and Windows, [per Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diablo_IV:_Lord_of_Hatred).

The headline additions are two new classes. The Paladin returns to the franchise wielding sword, shield, and divine auras, while the Warlock channels demonic minions and transformations against Mephisto's forces, [Blizzard's reveal post explains](https://news.blizzard.com/en-us/article/24267729/prepare-for-the-reckoning-lord-of-hatred-draws-near). The [official expansion page](https://diablo4.blizzard.com/en-us/lord-of-hatred) lists four specializations per class — "The Arbiter," "The Zealot," "The Judicator," and "The Juggernaut" for the Paladin, and "Legion," "Vanguard," "Mastermind," and "Ritualist" for the Warlock.

The new Skovos region serves as both campaign setting and endgame hub, anchored by the city of Temis. Blizzard describes it as "the ancient birthplace of the firstborn civilization," now ruled by The Oracle and an Amazon Queen, [according to the official page](https://diablo4.blizzard.com/en-us/lord-of-hatred). The narrative follows the Wanderer and Lorath as they confront Mephisto, the Prime Evil of Hatred, [Wikipedia summarizes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diablo_IV:_Lord_of_Hatred).

## The Endgame Rework

The most consequential change is structural. *Lord of Hatred* introduces War Plans, a system that lets players chain together endgame activities — Nightmare Dungeons, Helltides, Undercity, Lair Bosses, Infernal Hordes, and the Pit — into a single customizable progression path with selectable modifiers, [Blizzard's announcement details](https://news.blizzard.com/en-us/article/24267729/prepare-for-the-reckoning-lord-of-hatred-draws-near). The expansion also revives the Horadric Cube as a crafting station for transmutation, affix rerolling, and the new Talisman items, which use Seals and Charms to introduce set bonuses to *Diablo IV* for the first time. A new Echoing Hatred mode pits players against "unending demonic hordes" via rare Trace of Echoes drops, and Fishing arrives as a slower-paced activity at Skovos beaches and lakes.

Underpinning all of this is patch 3.0.0, which applies to every *Diablo IV* player whether or not they own the expansion. The level cap rises to 70, the four-tier difficulty system expands to 12 Torment levels, skill trees are completely redesigned across all classes with what Blizzard describes as "over 40 reworked choices, 80 additional options," and a comprehensive Loot Filter ships in the base game, [Blizzard confirmed](https://news.blizzard.com/en-us/article/24267729/prepare-for-the-reckoning-lord-of-hatred-draws-near). A community challenge titled "Hatred's Downfall" requires players to collectively earn 266.6 million Paragon Points to unlock a Crown of Hatred cosmetic.

## Critical Reception

Review aggregator scores settled into the generally favorable range. As of launch week, Metacritic shows an 84/100 Metascore on PC across 42 critic reviews, 84/100 on PlayStation 5 from 10 reviews, and 83/100 on Xbox Series X from five reviews, [per Metacritic's listings](https://www.metacritic.com/game/diablo-iv-lord-of-hatred/). The site reports 93% of professional reviews as positive, with 7% mixed and none negative.

User reception is more divided. Metacritic's PC user score sits at 6.8 from 69 ratings, with 65% positive and 28% negative responses, [the same Metacritic page shows](https://www.metacritic.com/game/diablo-iv-lord-of-hatred/). Common user criticisms cite excessive cutscenes, system complexity, and endgame reliance on recycled content, while praise focuses on campaign narrative and audiovisual polish.

## What We Don't Know

Blizzard has not yet disclosed first-week sales figures or active player counts on Battle.net. Unit sales for the expansion across consoles and storefronts will not appear until later trade reports, and any comparison to the 2024 *Vessel of Hatred* opening will require Blizzard's quarterly disclosures from parent company Microsoft. Long-term reception of the War Plans endgame structure will depend on how the Season of Reckoning unfolds over the coming three months.

The expansion's PC version is also the first major *Diablo IV* release fully available through Steam alongside Battle.net, but Blizzard has not broken out platform-level sales splits.

## Context

*Lord of Hatred* lands at a moment of broader turbulence in the gaming industry. As [previously reported](/article/2026-04/03-sony-raises-ps5-prices-up-to-150-worldwide-as-memory-chip-shortage-and-tariffs-squeeze-the-console-market) by The Machine Herald, console hardware costs have risen sharply in 2026 amid memory chip shortages and tariff pressures, and Microsoft-owned Blizzard launched the expansion directly into that headwind. The 3.0.0 patch's universal changes — particularly the loot filter and skill tree rework — represent Blizzard's most significant base-game overhaul since *Diablo IV*'s 2023 release, suggesting a continued shift toward live-service maintenance rather than discrete expansion content.
